```{=latex}
\newpage

\hypertarget{abstract}{\chapter*{Abstract}\label{abstract}}
\addcontentsline{toc}{chapter}{Abstract}
```

::: content-hidden
TODO: update and finalize the abstract
:::

This research rigorously assesses the effectiveness of distinct augmented instructional techniques within the framework of contemporary manufacturing training methods. A cohort of 62 participants, with varying prior exposures to assembly tasks but no prior experience with augmented or mixed reality, engaged in a simulated car assembly task at the Tiger Motors Lean Education Lab. Participants were introduced to different instructional methods: traditional Paper Work Instructions, Projected Augmented Reality, head-mounted Augmented Reality, and head-mounted Mixed Reality. For the purposes of this study, Mixed Reality is differentiated from Augmented by the participant's freedom to manipulate the workpiece while augmentations remain aligned.

Primary performance metrics were carefully documented, including task duration, error rates, and error classifications, providing insights into learning progression and task proficiency. In the recall phase, participants were tasked to replicate the assembly four times, relying solely on paper work instructions. Through video analysis, the frequency and duration of their references to those instructions were recorded, shedding light on the learning efficacy of each instructional method. Several weeks post-intervention, retention---how adeptly and promptly a participant could reproduce the assembly without additional instruction---was assessed among a volunteer subset.

Following both phases of the initial experiment, the NASA Task Load Index and System Usability Scale instruments, chosen for their reputable assessment of perceived workload and user experience, were administered. That data, coupled with participant demographics and qualitative feedback elicited through open-ended exit interviews, contributes to a comprehensive expression of each participant's experience.

Ultimately, this study explores the potential advantages and challenges of AR-based instructional methods. While the primary application lies in diverse manufacturing training and support, its implications extend to analogous tasks, including equipment installation, field repair, quality inspection, and safety training.
